prompt: In John 17, what does Jesus say eternal life is?
choices_json: Knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom he sent
Receiving glory before the world existed in this chapter
Being taken out of the world immediately
Keeping every tradition without fail
correct_answer: Knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom he sent
explanation: Jesus defines eternal life as knowing the Father and the Son he sent (John 17:3).

prompt: At the start of John 17, what does Jesus ask the Father to do for the Son?
choices_json: Glorify your Son
Hide your Son from the rulers
Send Elijah before your Son
Give your Son twelve legions of angels
correct_answer: Glorify your Son
explanation: Jesus begins the prayer by asking the Father to glorify the Son (John 17:1).

prompt: In John 17:9, who does Jesus say he is praying for?
choices_json: Those whom the Father has given him
The whole world without distinction in that moment
Only the rulers in Jerusalem
Only John and Peter by name
correct_answer: Those whom the Father has given him
explanation: Jesus says, 'I pray for them,' meaning those the Father gave him (John 17:9).

prompt: According to John 17:8, what had Jesus given the disciples?
choices_json: The words the Father gave him
A throne over Israel
Lanterns, torches, and weapons
A law written on stone tablets
correct_answer: The words the Father gave him
explanation: Jesus says he gave the disciples the words the Father gave him (John 17:8).

prompt: In John 17:15, from whom does Jesus ask the Father to keep the disciples?
choices_json: The evil one
The Roman governor
The stormy sea
The synagogue rulers only
correct_answer: The evil one
explanation: Jesus asks that the Father keep them from the evil one (John 17:15).

prompt: According to John 17:17, what is truth?
choices_json: Your word
The temple curtain
The crowd's opinion
The judgment seat
correct_answer: Your word
explanation: Jesus says to the Father, 'Your word is truth' (John 17:17).

prompt: In John 17, what does Jesus repeatedly pray believers will be?
choices_json: One
Hidden
Wealthy
Feared by every nation
correct_answer: One
explanation: Jesus prays that his people may be one as the Father and Son are one (John 17:11, 21-22).

prompt: What work does Jesus say he has accomplished on earth in John 17:4?
choices_json: The work the Father gave him to do
The overthrow of the Roman garrison
The final judgment of the world
The rebuilding of the temple courts
correct_answer: The work the Father gave him to do
explanation: Jesus says he accomplished the work the Father gave him (John 17:4).

prompt: What kind of glory does Jesus ask to receive again in John 17:5?
choices_json: The glory he had with the Father before the world existed
The glory of being crowned by the crowds in Jerusalem in this chapter's scene
The glory of ruling from David's earthly throne
The glory of the temple's gold and stones
correct_answer: The glory he had with the Father before the world existed
explanation: Jesus asks for the glory he shared with the Father before the world existed (John 17:5).

prompt: Who is the one lost among those given to Jesus in John 17:12?
choices_json: The son of destruction
The disciple whom Jesus loved
The servant named Malchus
The woman at the door
correct_answer: The son of destruction
explanation: Jesus says none were lost except 'the son of destruction' (John 17:12).

prompt: What does Jesus want made full in the disciples in John 17:13?
choices_json: His joy
Their political influence
Their secrecy from the world
Their freedom from all suffering
correct_answer: His joy
explanation: Jesus says he speaks so that his joy may be made full in them (John 17:13).

prompt: How does Jesus describe the disciples' relation to the world in John 17:14-16?
choices_json: They are not of the world
They will rule every kingdom of the world
They belong only to the temple courts
They are unknown to the Father
correct_answer: They are not of the world
explanation: Jesus says the disciples are not of the world, just as he is not of the world (John 17:14-16).

prompt: For whose sake does Jesus say he sanctifies himself in John 17:19?
choices_json: For the disciples' sake
For Pilate's sake alone
For the crowd's entertainment
For the soldiers' promotion
correct_answer: For the disciples' sake
explanation: Jesus says, 'For their sakes I sanctify myself' (John 17:19).

prompt: What does Jesus desire those given to him will see in John 17:24?
choices_json: His glory
The Ark of the Covenant
A sign from heaven at noon
A rebuilt earthly sanctuary
correct_answer: His glory
explanation: Jesus wants those given him to be with him and see his glory (John 17:24).

prompt: When Jesus says, 'I don't pray that you would take them from the world,' what situation is he assuming for the disciples?
choices_json: They must remain in the world while being guarded in it
They should leave every city and live in the wilderness
They are about to rule the world by force
They no longer need any protection at all
correct_answer: They must remain in the world while being guarded in it
explanation: Jesus expects the disciples to stay in the world but be kept from the evil one (John 17:15).

prompt: According to John 17:21, why does Jesus want believers to be one?
choices_json: So that the world may believe the Father sent him
So that the disciples can avoid all public witness
So that Rome will hand power to them
So that no one will ever oppose them again
correct_answer: So that the world may believe the Father sent him
explanation: Jesus links the believers' unity to the world's belief that the Father sent him (John 17:21).

prompt: In John 17:8, what convinced the disciples that Jesus came from the Father?
choices_json: They received his words and believed the Father sent him
They saw him seize power in Jerusalem
They heard Pilate declare him innocent
They watched the soldiers divide garments in this chapter's scene
correct_answer: They received his words and believed the Father sent him
explanation: Jesus says the disciples received his words and believed that he came from the Father (John 17:8).

prompt: When Jesus says the world hated the disciples, what reason does he give?
choices_json: They are not of the world, just as he is not of it
They refused to pay the temple tax that year
They were armed more strongly than the soldiers at that point in the chapter
They had hidden themselves in the Praetorium
correct_answer: They are not of the world, just as he is not of it
explanation: The world's hatred is tied to the disciples not being of the world (John 17:14).

prompt: In John 17:18-19, why does Jesus send and sanctify the disciples?
choices_json: So they may be consecrated for the mission into the world
So they can withdraw forever from public life in that moment of the story
So they can sit on thrones before the cross
So they may judge Pilate before sunrise
correct_answer: So they may be consecrated for the mission into the world
explanation: Jesus ties sanctification to being sent into the world as he was sent (John 17:18-19).

prompt: Who else does Jesus include in this prayer beyond the men already with him?
choices_json: Those who will believe through the disciples' word
Only the priests serving in the temple that week within this part of John
Only Jesus' relatives from Nazareth
Only rulers who later change sides
correct_answer: Those who will believe through the disciples' word
explanation: Jesus expands the prayer to future believers who will believe through the disciples' word (John 17:20).

prompt: At the end of John 17, what is the goal of Jesus making the Father's name known?
choices_json: That the Father's love may be in them, and Jesus in them
That the disciples may never have to suffer again in this chapter
That every nation will crown them immediately
That the temple veil will be repaired by them
correct_answer: That the Father's love may be in them, and Jesus in them
explanation: Jesus says he made the Father's name known so that divine love and his presence may be in them (John 17:26).

prompt: When Jesus asks for the glory he had 'before the world existed,' what does that highlight about him in John 17?
choices_json: His shared preexistent life with the Father
His desire for political honor from Rome
His need to earn sonship for the first time
His plan to rebuild Solomon's temple personally
correct_answer: His shared preexistent life with the Father
explanation: The prayer speaks of glory shared with the Father before the world existed (John 17:5).

prompt: How does John 17 define eternal life?
choices_json: As relational knowledge of the Father and the Son he sent
As endless survival apart from communion with God in that moment of the story
As escape from the physical world by secrecy
As national victory over hostile rulers
correct_answer: As relational knowledge of the Father and the Son he sent
explanation: John 17 defines eternal life as knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom he sent (John 17:3).

prompt: In John 17, the disciples are 'not of the world' yet sent into it. What tension does that create?
choices_json: They must live as a distinct people while remaining on mission within the world
They must choose between speaking truth and obeying Jesus at all within this part of John
They should seize worldly rule even while denying any mission
They are meant to stay hidden until the temple leaders call them
correct_answer: They must live as a distinct people while remaining on mission within the world
explanation: Jesus says they are not of the world, yet he sends them into the world (John 17:16-18).

prompt: Why is unity in John 17 modeled on the Father and the Son rather than mere agreement?
choices_json: Because the prayer aims at a shared life that reflects divine communion
Because Jesus only wants the disciples to vote the same way in this chapter's scene
Because the chapter is mainly about military coordination
Because John 17 replaces truth with outward harmony
correct_answer: Because the prayer aims at a shared life that reflects divine communion
explanation: Jesus prays for oneness patterned after the Father-Son relationship (John 17:21-23).

prompt: What connection does John 17 make between God's word and the disciples' sanctification?
choices_json: God's truthful word is the means by which they are sanctified
God's word is set aside once the disciples are sent in this chapter
God's word is replaced by Roman legal verdicts
God's word matters only for priests, not disciples
correct_answer: God's truthful word is the means by which they are sanctified
explanation: Jesus prays, 'Sanctify them in your truth. Your word is truth' (John 17:17).

prompt: How does the prayer widen in John 17:20?
choices_json: It moves from the present disciples to future believers through their witness
It turns from prayer to a legal defense before the high priest in that moment of the story
It narrows from the twelve to a single favored disciple
It changes from earthly concerns to temple architecture
correct_answer: It moves from the present disciples to future believers through their witness
explanation: Jesus explicitly includes those who will believe through the disciples' word (John 17:20).

prompt: What does Jesus' request that believers be with him where he is add to the prayer?
choices_json: A hope of final fellowship in his presence and glory
A request that they avoid every earthly responsibility
A promise that they will never die physically
A command that they must all move to Galilee immediately
correct_answer: A hope of final fellowship in his presence and glory
explanation: Jesus desires that his people be with him and behold his glory (John 17:24).

prompt: In John 17:22-23, why does Jesus give believers the glory the Father gave him?
choices_json: So that they may be brought into visible unity before the world
So that they can overthrow the nations by force
So that they can avoid speaking the Father's word in this chapter's scene
So that they may replace the Son in the prayer
correct_answer: So that they may be brought into visible unity before the world
explanation: Jesus says he gives them glory so they may be one and the world may know the Father sent him (John 17:22-23).

prompt: What does the mention of 'the son of destruction' emphasize inside Jesus' prayer?
choices_json: That even the loss of Judas fits the fulfillment of Scripture
That the disciples were mostly abandoned by Jesus
That Rome had gained final control over the mission
That Jesus regretted giving anyone the Father's word in this chapter
correct_answer: That even the loss of Judas fits the fulfillment of Scripture
explanation: Jesus says the one lost fulfilled Scripture, even as the others were kept (John 17:12).

prompt: How does John 17 present Jesus' coming death even before the arrest begins in chapter 18?
choices_json: As a consecrating, glory-filled mission he receives from the Father
As an accident caused by Peter's lack of caution in that moment of the story
As a secret plan hidden from the Father himself
As a retreat from the world that cancels witness
correct_answer: As a consecrating, glory-filled mission he receives from the Father
explanation: Jesus speaks of finishing the Father's work, sanctifying himself, and returning to glory (John 17:4-5, 19).

prompt: Why is 'knowing ... the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you sent' more than bare information in John 17?
choices_json: Because Jesus presents it as the lived reality of salvation itself
Because it refers only to memorizing the right vocabulary
Because it means avoiding any future obedience or witness
Because it is limited to the priests already inside the temple within this part of John
correct_answer: Because Jesus presents it as the lived reality of salvation itself
explanation: Jesus does not treat knowledge as trivia but as eternal life itself (John 17:3).

prompt: What picture of indwelling appears in the line 'I in them, and you in me'?
choices_json: A chain of shared divine life joining believers to the Father through the Son
A political alliance joining Judea to Rome through Pilate in this chapter's scene
A family tree proving only physical descent from Abraham
A temple ritual performed once a year by the high priest
correct_answer: A chain of shared divine life joining believers to the Father through the Son
explanation: Jesus describes a communion in which he is in believers and the Father is in him (John 17:23).

prompt: Why does Jesus keep returning to the theme that the Father sent him?
choices_json: Because the chapter ties his identity, mission, and the world's response to that sending
Because he wants to avoid speaking about the Father directly
Because only Roman law can explain who he is
Because the disciples already understand everything without witness at that point in the chapter
correct_answer: Because the chapter ties his identity, mission, and the world's response to that sending
explanation: Jesus repeatedly speaks of being sent and links belief, unity, and knowledge of God to that sending (John 17:3, 8, 18, 21, 23, 25).

prompt: How does John 17 end the prayer's movement from revelation to communion?
choices_json: With the Father's love in believers and Jesus himself in them
With the disciples seated on earthly thrones in Jerusalem in that moment of the story
With a command to build a new sanctuary before dawn
With the crowd finally agreeing to every statement
correct_answer: With the Father's love in believers and Jesus himself in them
explanation: The prayer closes with love and indwelling as the goal of revelation (John 17:26).

prompt: After speaking these words, what brook did Jesus cross in John 18?
choices_json: The brook Kidron
The Jordan River
The Sea of Galilee
The Pool of Bethesda
correct_answer: The brook Kidron
explanation: Jesus went over the brook Kidron into a garden with his disciples (John 18:1).

prompt: Who arrived at the garden with soldiers and officers in John 18:3?
choices_json: Judas
Thomas
Nicodemus
Joseph of Arimathaea
correct_answer: Judas
explanation: Judas came with a detachment of soldiers and officers to the garden (John 18:3).

prompt: What happened when Jesus said, 'I am he,' in the garden?
choices_json: The arresting party went backward and fell to the ground
The high priest tore his garments in the court
Peter ran away into the city at once
Pilate came out from the Praetorium immediately in this chapter
correct_answer: The arresting party went backward and fell to the ground
explanation: When Jesus said, 'I am he,' they went backward and fell to the ground (John 18:6).

prompt: Whose ear did Peter cut off in John 18?
choices_json: Malchus's
Caiaphas's
Annas's
Barabbas's
correct_answer: Malchus's
explanation: Peter struck the high priest's servant and cut off the ear of Malchus (John 18:10).

prompt: What were the servants and officers standing around while Peter warmed himself?
choices_json: A fire of coals
A bronze altar
A Roman standard
A stone water jar
correct_answer: A fire of coals
explanation: Peter stood warming himself by a fire of coals because it was cold (John 18:18).

prompt: Who asked, 'What accusation do you bring against this man?' in John 18:29?
choices_json: Pilate
Annas
Peter
Barabbas
correct_answer: Pilate
explanation: Pilate asked the accusers what accusation they brought against Jesus (John 18:29).

prompt: Whom did the crowd ask Pilate to release instead of Jesus?
choices_json: Barabbas
Malchus
Lazarus
Nicodemus
correct_answer: Barabbas
explanation: The crowd cried out for Barabbas instead of Jesus (John 18:40).

prompt: When Jesus was first taken from the garden, to whom was he led first?
choices_json: Annas
Pilate
Barabbas
Joseph of Arimathaea
correct_answer: Annas
explanation: Jesus was led first to Annas (John 18:13).

prompt: How was Annas related to Caiaphas in John 18:13?
choices_json: He was Caiaphas's father-in-law
He was Caiaphas's brother within this part of John
He was Caiaphas's son
He was Caiaphas's servant
correct_answer: He was Caiaphas's father-in-law
explanation: John notes that Annas was father-in-law to Caiaphas (John 18:13).

prompt: What did Peter say when the maid at the door asked if he was one of Jesus' disciples?
choices_json: I am not
I am he
You say that I am
What is truth?
correct_answer: I am not
explanation: Peter answered the maid, 'I am not' (John 18:17).

prompt: According to John 18:20, where had Jesus taught openly?
choices_json: In synagogues and in the temple
Only inside a locked upper room
Only in the Praetorium at dawn
Only in the desert beyond the Jordan
correct_answer: In synagogues and in the temple
explanation: Jesus says he taught openly in synagogues and in the temple (John 18:20).

prompt: Who challenged Peter by saying he had seen him in the garden?
choices_json: A relative of the man whose ear Peter had cut off
The disciple whom Jesus loved
Pilate's wife from the governor's house in this chapter
A merchant from the temple court
correct_answer: A relative of the man whose ear Peter had cut off
explanation: A relative of Malchus asked Peter if he had seen him in the garden with Jesus (John 18:26).

prompt: Why did Jesus' accusers stay outside the Praetorium in John 18:28?
choices_json: They did not want to be defiled before eating the Passover
They were waiting for sunrise to identify Jesus within this part of John
They feared the soldiers would arrest them all
They had already entered and been sent back out
correct_answer: They did not want to be defiled before eating the Passover
explanation: They stayed outside so they would not be defiled and could eat the Passover (John 18:28).

prompt: What custom does Pilate mention at the Passover in John 18:39?
choices_json: Releasing one prisoner to the people
Changing the high priest every year
Casting lots for a criminal's clothing
Opening the temple treasury to the poor
correct_answer: Releasing one prisoner to the people
explanation: Pilate refers to the custom of releasing someone at the Passover (John 18:39).

prompt: What does Jesus' stepping forward and asking 'Who are you looking for?' show in the arrest scene?
choices_json: He meets the arresting party deliberately rather than being cornered by surprise
He is unsure why the soldiers came to the garden that night
He is trying to escape while the disciples distract the crowd at that point in the chapter
He wants Judas to explain the charges before speaking
correct_answer: He meets the arresting party deliberately rather than being cornered by surprise
explanation: John says Jesus knew what was happening and then stepped forward to address them (John 18:4).

prompt: Why does Jesus tell the arresting party, 'Let these go their way'?
choices_json: To preserve his disciples in fulfillment of his earlier word
To give the disciples time to attack with swords in that moment of the story
To send the disciples into the Praetorium first
To make the officers arrest Judas instead
correct_answer: To preserve his disciples in fulfillment of his earlier word
explanation: John says this happened so that Jesus' word about losing none of those given him would be fulfilled (John 18:8-9).

prompt: How does Jesus interpret Peter's sword strike in John 18:11?
choices_json: As resistance that must give way to the cup the Father has given him
As the beginning of a justified uprising against Rome within this part of John
As proof that the kingdom has now become worldly
As a necessary act because the Father abandoned him
correct_answer: As resistance that must give way to the cup the Father has given him
explanation: Jesus tells Peter to sheathe the sword and speaks of drinking the Father's cup (John 18:11).

prompt: What contrast does John 18 create by alternating Peter's denials with Jesus' testimony?
choices_json: Peter shrinks from identification while Jesus speaks openly under pressure
Peter speaks publicly while Jesus remains entirely silent throughout in this chapter
Peter and Jesus both answer in exactly the same words each time
Peter denies Jesus only after Pilate releases Barabbas
correct_answer: Peter shrinks from identification while Jesus speaks openly under pressure
explanation: Peter says, 'I am not,' while Jesus insists he has spoken openly to the world (John 18:17, 20, 25-27).

prompt: Why is the leaders' refusal to enter the Praetorium ironic in the chapter?
choices_json: They guard ritual cleanness while pressing for Jesus' death
They honor Jesus publicly while debating legal details
They refuse Roman help because they want mercy instead
They have already decided to release Jesus before breakfast
correct_answer: They guard ritual cleanness while pressing for Jesus' death
explanation: John highlights their concern about defilement even as they deliver Jesus over to be killed (John 18:28-31).

prompt: In Jesus' answer to Pilate, how would his servants act if his kingdom were from this world?
choices_json: They would fight so he would not be handed over
They would remain silent and hide in the temple
They would ask Barabbas to speak for them
They would insist on being judged only by Annas
correct_answer: They would fight so he would not be handed over
explanation: Jesus says worldly servants would fight to prevent his delivery to the Jews (John 18:36).

prompt: What does Pilate's 'What is truth?' followed by 'I find no basis for a charge' suggest about him in John 18?
choices_json: He brushes against Jesus' claim about truth without really receiving it
He fully confesses Jesus as Lord and God on the spot
He understands the kingdom perfectly and joins it immediately within this part of John
He has not yet heard any testimony about Jesus at all
correct_answer: He brushes against Jesus' claim about truth without really receiving it
explanation: Pilate hears Jesus speak of truth yet turns back to political handling of the case (John 18:37-38).

prompt: What is the literary effect of the soldiers falling back at Jesus' words in John 18?
choices_json: It shows that the one being arrested still acts with commanding authority
It proves the soldiers mistook Jesus for Barabbas in the dark in this chapter's scene
It signals that the arrest has failed and will not continue
It means Judas immediately repented before everyone present
correct_answer: It shows that the one being arrested still acts with commanding authority
explanation: The arrest scene briefly reveals Jesus' authority even before he is bound (John 18:5-6, 12).

prompt: How does John 18 portray Jesus even while he is being arrested and bound?
choices_json: As the sovereign figure who directs the scene and speaks with purpose
As someone confused about why the crowd has arrived
As a victim whose words no longer carry any force at all at that point in the chapter
As a hidden bystander to Peter's real mission
correct_answer: As the sovereign figure who directs the scene and speaks with purpose
explanation: Jesus knows what is coming, protects the disciples, and addresses both priest and governor directly (John 18:4, 8, 20, 36-37).

prompt: What does 'My kingdom is not of this world' deny in John 18?
choices_json: That Jesus' rule originates from worldly power and violence
That Jesus has any kingdom or authority whatsoever in this chapter
That truth matters in public life before rulers
That Israel has any need for a Messiah at all
correct_answer: That Jesus' rule originates from worldly power and violence
explanation: Jesus does not deny being a king; he denies that his kingdom comes from this world (John 18:36-37).

prompt: In John 18, what kind of witness does Jesus claim for his teaching?
choices_json: Open public testimony that hearers can verify
A hidden secret known only to one disciple
A coded message passed only to priests at night
A private vision Pilate alone is meant to interpret
correct_answer: Open public testimony that hearers can verify
explanation: Jesus says he taught openly and tells the high priest to ask those who heard him (John 18:20-21).

prompt: Why is Barabbas a pointed substitute in the release scene?
choices_json: The crowd prefers a robber over the innocent king standing before them
Barabbas is the high priest's chosen witness for Jesus' defense in this chapter
Barabbas has already testified that Jesus is the truth
The crowd mistakes Barabbas for one of Jesus' disciples
correct_answer: The crowd prefers a robber over the innocent king standing before them
explanation: John notes that Barabbas was a robber, sharpening the exchange (John 18:38-40).

prompt: How does the note about 'what kind of death he should die' tie the Roman trial to Jesus' own words?
choices_json: It shows the handover to Pilate fulfills Jesus' prior prediction of his manner of death
It explains why Peter must die before the morning trial begins
It means the Jewish leaders now plan to stone Jesus inside the temple at that point in the chapter
It proves Pilate has become one of Jesus' disciples secretly
correct_answer: It shows the handover to Pilate fulfills Jesus' prior prediction of his manner of death
explanation: John says the appeal to Roman authority happened so Jesus' word about how he would die might be fulfilled (John 18:31-32).

prompt: What does Peter's third denial ending with the rooster crow underscore in John 18?
choices_json: The collapse of his confidence under pressure
The public vindication of his bravery before the crowd
The moment he begins preaching to the officers boldly
The instant Pilate releases Jesus from custody
correct_answer: The collapse of his confidence under pressure
explanation: Peter denies Jesus again, and immediately the rooster crows (John 18:27).

prompt: What contrast between Jesus' 'I am he' and Peter's 'I am not' drives John 18?
choices_json: Jesus owns his identity while Peter disowns his association under pressure
Jesus and Peter both deny themselves in exactly the same way
Peter speaks with divine authority while Jesus avoids all self-reference in this chapter
The contrast disappears once Pilate asks his first question
correct_answer: Jesus owns his identity while Peter disowns his association under pressure
explanation: The chapter repeatedly places Jesus' self-identification beside Peter's denials (John 18:5, 17, 25).

prompt: How does John 18 join kingship and truth in Jesus' exchange with Pilate?
choices_json: Jesus presents his kingship as bound up with bearing witness to the truth
Jesus rejects kingship so he can speak only as a philosopher
Pilate crowns Jesus because truth has already triumphed politically in this chapter
The chapter separates truth from public authority completely
correct_answer: Jesus presents his kingship as bound up with bearing witness to the truth
explanation: Jesus answers Pilate's question about kingship by speaking of his mission to testify to the truth (John 18:37).

prompt: What does Jesus' protection of the disciples during the arrest reveal about his mission in John 18?
choices_json: Even in surrender, he is still guarding those the Father gave him
He has decided to abandon the disciples to save himself at that point in the chapter
He now trusts Peter's sword more than the Father's will
He needs the disciples to begin the trial in his place
correct_answer: Even in surrender, he is still guarding those the Father gave him
explanation: Jesus' command to let the disciples go fulfills his word that he lost none given to him (John 18:8-9).

prompt: What does the garden setting contribute to Judas's betrayal in John 18?
choices_json: It shows betrayal invading a familiar place of fellowship with Jesus
It proves Judas was unsure where Jesus usually spent the night in that moment of the story
It turns the arrest into a random encounter on a public road
It means none of the disciples had ever been there before
correct_answer: It shows betrayal invading a familiar place of fellowship with Jesus
explanation: Judas knew the place because Jesus often met there with his disciples (John 18:2).

prompt: Why does John show Jesus speaking openly while his accusers rely on vague charges?
choices_json: To contrast transparent truth with evasive injustice
To prove the priests have quoted every sermon perfectly
To show Pilate and the priests already agree on every fact
To make Peter, rather than Jesus, the main defendant
correct_answer: To contrast transparent truth with evasive injustice
explanation: Jesus says he spoke openly, but the leaders offer broad accusation instead of clear testimony (John 18:20-21, 29-30).

prompt: What is exposed by the leaders seeking ritual cleanness while delivering Jesus to death?
choices_json: A hollow purity that masks moral corruption
A faithful obedience that fully understands Jesus' mission
A complete willingness to hear the truth from Jesus
A consistent refusal to use Roman power for any purpose
correct_answer: A hollow purity that masks moral corruption
explanation: Their concern about defilement is set against their role in seeking Jesus' execution (John 18:28-31).

prompt: In the Barabbas exchange, what kind of king does the crowd reject?
choices_json: The truthful, innocent king whose kingdom is not from this world
A violent rebel stronger than Barabbas in armed revolt at that point in the chapter
A silent mystic who refuses every public witness
A temple priest chosen by Caesar for the feast
correct_answer: The truthful, innocent king whose kingdom is not from this world
explanation: The release scene rejects the one Pilate calls 'the King of the Jews' even after declaring him innocent (John 18:36-40).

prompt: What did the soldiers twist together and place on Jesus' head in John 19?
choices_json: A crown of thorns
A wreath of olive branches
A golden diadem
A linen turban
correct_answer: A crown of thorns
explanation: The soldiers twisted thorns into a crown and put it on Jesus' head (John 19:2).

prompt: What color garment did the soldiers put on Jesus in John 19:2?
choices_json: Purple
Scarlet
White
Black
correct_answer: Purple
explanation: The soldiers dressed Jesus in a purple garment (John 19:2).

prompt: What did Pilate say when he presented Jesus wearing the crown and robe in John 19:5?
choices_json: Behold, the man!
Behold, your King!
What is truth?
I am thirsty.
correct_answer: Behold, the man!
explanation: Pilate says, 'Behold, the man!' when Jesus comes out wearing the crown and purple garment (John 19:5).

prompt: What Hebrew place name does John 19 use for 'The Place of a Skull'?
choices_json: Golgotha
Gabbatha

prompt: Who first said, 'It's the Lord!' in John 21?
choices_json: The disciple whom Jesus loved
Simon Peter
Thomas called Didymus
Nathanael of Cana
correct_answer: The disciple whom Jesus loved
explanation: The disciple whom Jesus loved recognizes Jesus first and tells Peter, 'It's the Lord!' (John 21:7).

prompt: How many fish were in the net in John 21:11?
explanation: Peter drew in a net full of 153 great fish (John 21:11).

prompt: What meal invitation did Jesus give the disciples on the shore?
choices_json: Come and eat breakfast!
Come and keep Passover!
Come and warm yourselves!
Come and see the garden!
correct_answer: Come and eat breakfast!
explanation: Jesus invites the disciples, 'Come and eat breakfast!' (John 21:12).

prompt: What command did Jesus give Peter after speaking about the death by which Peter would glorify God?
choices_json: Follow me.
Wait here.
Fear not.
Go home.
correct_answer: Follow me.
explanation: After speaking about Peter's future death, Jesus says, 'Follow me' (John 21:19).

prompt: Which named disciple in the fishing group was from Cana in Galilee?
choices_json: Nathanael
Thomas
Andrew
Philip
correct_answer: Nathanael
explanation: John identifies Nathanael as being from Cana in Galilee (John 21:2).

prompt: About how far from land were the disciples when they came in the little boat?
choices_json: About two hundred cubits
About fifty cubits
About one thousand cubits
About a Sabbath day's journey
correct_answer: About two hundred cubits
explanation: The disciples were about two hundred cubits from land (John 21:8).

prompt: What did Peter wrap around himself before jumping into the sea?
choices_json: His coat
A purple garment
A linen cloth
A leather belt only
correct_answer: His coat
explanation: Peter wrapped his coat around himself and threw himself into the sea (John 21:7).

prompt: What did the disciples find already prepared on the shore in John 21:9?
choices_json: A fire of coals with fish and bread
A table with wine and bitter herbs
A basket of figs beside a stone jar
A pile of nets and an empty boat
correct_answer: A fire of coals with fish and bread
explanation: When they reached land, they saw a fire of coals with fish and bread laid on it (John 21:9).

prompt: What happened to the net despite the great number of fish?
choices_json: It was not torn
It broke in two places
It sank beneath the boat
It drifted away from the shore
correct_answer: It was not torn
explanation: Even with many fish, the net was not torn (John 21:11).

prompt: What was Jesus' second response to Peter's affirmation of love in John 21?
choices_json: Tend my sheep.
Feed my lambs.
Feed my sheep.
Cast the net again.
correct_answer: Tend my sheep.
explanation: After Peter's second affirmation, Jesus says, 'Tend my sheep' (John 21:16).

prompt: What rumor spread among the brothers about the beloved disciple in John 21:23?
choices_json: That he would not die
That he would become the next high priest
That he had counted the fish incorrectly
That he had written the inscription on the cross
correct_answer: That he would not die
explanation: A saying spread that the beloved disciple would not die, though John clarifies that Jesus had not said this (John 21:23).

prompt: What does the huge catch after an empty night show in John 21?
choices_json: Fruitfulness comes through obeying the risen Jesus' word
Fishing skill alone is enough once daylight comes
Peter succeeds only when he ignores the other disciples
The sea itself recognizes Peter as the new leader
correct_answer: Fruitfulness comes through obeying the risen Jesus' word
explanation: The disciples catch nothing on their own, then gather many fish at Jesus' command (John 21:3-6).

prompt: How do the beloved disciple and Peter respond differently once Jesus is recognized?
choices_json: One discerns and speaks first, while the other rushes toward Jesus impulsively
One denies Jesus again, while the other goes back to sleep in the boat
Both remain silent until Jesus drags the net ashore alone
Both immediately leave the fish and walk home without speaking
correct_answer: One discerns and speaks first, while the other rushes toward Jesus impulsively
explanation: The beloved disciple recognizes Jesus first, and Peter responds by jumping into the sea (John 21:7).

prompt: Why is the breakfast scene an apt setting for Peter's restoration in John 21?
choices_json: It restores fellowship before Jesus entrusts Peter with shepherding work
It keeps Peter far from Jesus so he cannot speak openly
It is where Pilate returns to review the charges against Peter
It replaces the need for Jesus to ask Peter anything at all
correct_answer: It restores fellowship before Jesus entrusts Peter with shepherding work
explanation: Jesus feeds the disciples first, then turns to Peter with the repeated questions and charges (John 21:12-17).

prompt: What does Jesus' threefold question, 'Do you love me?' echo in Peter's story?
choices_json: Peter's earlier three denials of Jesus
The three languages on the cross inscription
The three days Jesus spent in the tomb
The three women mentioned at the cross
correct_answer: Peter's earlier three denials of Jesus
explanation: Jesus asks Peter three times, matching the pattern of Peter's three denials earlier in the Gospel (John 21:15-17).

prompt: What does 'Follow me' mean in light of Peter's predicted death in John 21?
choices_json: Discipleship includes costly obedience that may lead to suffering
Peter should stop shepherding and return to fishing permanently
Peter will avoid death entirely if he obeys quickly enough
Following Jesus now means watching the beloved disciple instead
correct_answer: Discipleship includes costly obedience that may lead to suffering
explanation: Jesus links Peter's future death with the command, 'Follow me' (John 21:18-19).

prompt: Why does Jesus answer Peter's question about the beloved disciple with 'What is that to you?'
choices_json: He redirects Peter from comparison back to his own call to follow
He reveals that the beloved disciple is secretly replacing Peter
He refuses to speak because the disciples should know already
He announces that no disciple will ever die after the resurrection
correct_answer: He redirects Peter from comparison back to his own call to follow
explanation: Jesus answers Peter's question about the other disciple by saying, 'You follow me' (John 21:21-22).

prompt: What function does John 21:24 serve after the story about Peter and the beloved disciple?
choices_json: It identifies the beloved disciple as a trustworthy witness to these things
It transfers authorship of the Gospel to Peter instead
It explains how the disciples sold the fish in the marketplace
It announces a fourth appearance that the chapter then omits
correct_answer: It identifies the beloved disciple as a trustworthy witness to these things
explanation: John identifies the beloved disciple as the witness who wrote these things and whose testimony is true (John 21:24).

prompt: What does the untorn net full of 153 great fish suggest within John 21's scene?
choices_json: Abundant gathering under Jesus' direction without the mission falling apart
A coded prediction that exactly 153 disciples will remain faithful
A sign that the disciples should return to fishing as their main calling
A hidden rebuke proving Peter counted the catch incorrectly
correct_answer: Abundant gathering under Jesus' direction without the mission falling apart
explanation: The catch is abundant and yet the net is not torn, highlighting provision and cohesion under Jesus' word (John 21:6, 11).

prompt: How do the fire of coals and breakfast setting shape Jesus' restoration of Peter in John 21?
choices_json: They make the restoration intimate and gracious rather than merely judicial
They distance Jesus from Peter so no personal exchange can happen
They turn the scene into a public trial before a hostile crowd
They show Peter must earn another meal before answering Jesus
correct_answer: They make the restoration intimate and gracious rather than merely judicial
explanation: Jesus prepares a meal and then speaks personally with Peter about love and calling (John 21:9-17).

prompt: Why are love for Jesus and feeding his sheep joined together in John 21?
choices_json: Because devotion to Jesus is expressed through caring for the people who belong to him
Because Peter can choose either affection or obedience but not both
Because shepherding replaces the need to follow Jesus personally
Because the sheep are literal animals from the shoreline flock
correct_answer: Because devotion to Jesus is expressed through caring for the people who belong to him
explanation: Each affirmation of Peter's love is answered with a charge to feed or tend Jesus' flock (John 21:15-17).

prompt: What does John 21 suggest about the callings of Peter and the beloved disciple?
choices_json: They are distinct callings under the same Lord, not roles to be measured against each other
Only Peter has a true calling, while the beloved disciple simply observes
Both men receive identical futures and identical public tasks
The beloved disciple must replace Peter because Peter once denied Jesus
correct_answer: They are distinct callings under the same Lord, not roles to be measured against each other
explanation: Jesus tells Peter not to measure his path by the beloved disciple's future but to follow him himself (John 21:20-22).

prompt: Why does John mention that this was the third time Jesus was revealed to the disciples after rising?
choices_json: It underscores that these appearances form a real, ongoing post-resurrection witness
It implies Jesus had only appeared to Peter before this chapter
It proves the disciples still doubted every earlier appearance completely
It means this scene happened three times in exactly the same way
correct_answer: It underscores that these appearances form a real, ongoing post-resurrection witness
explanation: John notes this was the third appearance to the disciples after the resurrection (John 21:14).

prompt: What does the correction of the rumor about the beloved disciple show in John 21?
choices_json: Faithful testimony must preserve Jesus' actual words rather than exaggerating them
The brothers correctly understood Jesus and John later changed the story
Peter was the source of every rumor and had to be silenced
Jesus meant that no disciple would ever face death again
correct_answer: Faithful testimony must preserve Jesus' actual words rather than exaggerating them
explanation: John carefully explains that Jesus did not say the disciple would not die; he said, 'If I desire...' (John 21:22-23).

prompt: How does John 21:25 expand the horizon beyond the final shoreline scene?
choices_json: It says Jesus did many more things than this book could contain fully
It announces a second volume devoted only to fishing stories
It concludes that the resurrection appearances were probably legends
It says the world has already received every detail in written form
correct_answer: It says Jesus did many more things than this book could contain fully
explanation: The Gospel closes by saying that the world itself could not contain the books if everything Jesus did were written (John 21:25).

prompt: How does John 21 restore Peter publicly without pretending his denials never happened?
choices_json: Jesus answers Peter's threefold failure with a threefold question and renewed commission
Jesus avoids mentioning love or discipleship so Peter will not feel uncomfortable
Peter restores himself by recounting the number of fish before the others
The beloved disciple tells Peter that his earlier denials no longer matter
correct_answer: Jesus answers Peter's threefold failure with a threefold question and renewed commission
explanation: Peter is not ignored or replaced; he is restored through Jesus' repeated questioning and commission (John 21:15-17).

prompt: Why is Peter's future death described as a way to glorify God in John 21?
choices_json: Because faithful discipleship can honor God even through suffering and death
Because Peter will die before Jesus and thereby replace him as Savior
Because martyrdom automatically cancels Peter's need to follow Jesus
Because death is presented as greater than resurrection in the chapter
correct_answer: Because faithful discipleship can honor God even through suffering and death
explanation: John explains that Jesus' words about Peter's future signified the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God (John 21:18-19).

prompt: What deeper danger is Jesus addressing when Peter asks, 'Lord, what about this man?'
choices_json: The temptation to compare another disciple's path instead of obeying his own call
The risk that the beloved disciple might steal the fish from the net
The fear that Jesus has forgotten the number of earlier appearances
The possibility that the brothers will never hear the rumor about John
correct_answer: The temptation to compare another disciple's path instead of obeying his own call
explanation: Jesus refuses the comparison and returns Peter to the command, 'You follow me' (John 21:21-22).

prompt: How does the sea miracle prepare for the shepherding commission that follows in John 21?
choices_json: It shows that life-giving abundance comes from Jesus before Peter is told to feed the flock
It proves Peter's independent fishing talent is the basis of his later authority
It separates material provision from spiritual care as unrelated matters
It reveals the disciples should ignore Jesus until breakfast is finished
correct_answer: It shows that life-giving abundance comes from Jesus before Peter is told to feed the flock
explanation: Jesus provides the catch and the meal before he charges Peter to feed and tend his sheep (John 21:6, 9, 15-17).

prompt: What do John 21:24-25 claim about the Gospel's testimony?
choices_json: It is trustworthy witness to Jesus while still admitting Jesus' works exceed the book
It is a private draft that should not be trusted without Roman approval
It is complete only when the rumor about the beloved disciple is accepted
It is mainly a fishing memoir with little value beyond Galilee
correct_answer: It is trustworthy witness to Jesus while still admitting Jesus' works exceed the book
explanation: The closing lines affirm that the witness is true and that Jesus did far more than could be fully written down (John 21:24-25).

prompt: How does John 21 connect receiving Jesus' care with giving care to others?
choices_json: The disciples are fed by Jesus, and Peter is then told to feed Jesus' sheep
Peter feeds the crowd first, and Jesus later joins him for breakfast
The beloved disciple tends sheep while Jesus repairs the torn net
Thomas receives no care because he doubted in the previous chapter
correct_answer: The disciples are fed by Jesus, and Peter is then told to feed Jesus' sheep
explanation: Jesus first provides breakfast, then turns to Peter with the command to feed and tend his flock (John 21:12-17).

prompt: Why does John's Gospel end with both a trustworthy witness and an admission that Jesus' works exceed the book?
choices_json: It closes with confidence in the testimony while keeping Jesus' significance larger than the written account
It suggests the author doubts whether any resurrection appearance really happened
It means the earlier chapters should now be replaced by oral rumors alone
It leaves the reader with no solid basis for belief or discipleship
correct_answer: It closes with confidence in the testimony while keeping Jesus' significance larger than the written account
explanation: The last verses pair 'his witness is true' with the claim that Jesus did many more things than could be written (John 21:24-25).
